mission.title: Install and Configure Cortex on Kubernetes
mission.description: Cortex is a horizontally scalable, highly available, multi-tenant long-term storage solution for Prometheus and OpenTelemetry Metrics. Installing Cortex allows you to efficiently manage and store metrics data across multiple sources in a cluster environment.

steps.title: Add the Cortex Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Cortex Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add cortex https://cortexmetrics.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Cortex using Helm
steps.description:
Deploy Cortex to your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This command installs Cortex in the 'cortex' namespace:
```bash
helm install cortex cortex/cortex --version v1.20.1 --namespace cortex --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Cortex pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace cortex
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Set resource limits for the Cortex deployment by editing the Helm release configuration:
```bash
helm upgrade cortex cortex/cortex --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi --namespace cortex
```

steps.title: Set up monitoring
steps.description:
Enable monitoring for Cortex by configuring Prometheus to scrape metrics from the Cortex service. Modify your Prometheus configuration to include:
```yaml
- job_name: 'cortex'
  static_configs:
    - targets: ['cortex:9090']
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Cortex will show the Cortex pods running in the 'cortex' namespace. You can also verify that Prometheus is scraping metrics from the Cortex service as configured.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add cortex https://cortexmetrics.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
helm install cortex cortex/cortex --version v1.20.1 --namespace cortex --create-namespace
helm upgrade cortex cortex/cortex --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi --namespace cortex

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Cortex release from the Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This command will remove all resources associated with the release in the specified namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall cortex --namespace cortex
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were created during the installation. This command will delete the CRDs associated with Cortex:
```bash
kubectl delete crd cortex.*
```
Additionally, clean up any persistent volume claims (PVCs) if they were created:
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace cortex
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'cortex' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace cortex
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of the Cortex deployment. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
helm get values cortex --namespace cortex > cortex-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then upgrade the Cortex release to the desired version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade cortex cortex/cortex --version v1.21.0 --namespace cortex
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check the status of the Cortex pods to ensure they are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace cortex
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Cortex pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the failing pod to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace cortex
```
Common causes include misconfiguration or insufficient resources.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Cortex service, verify that the service is correctly configured and running:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace cortex
```
Ensure that the service endpoints are correctly pointing to the pods.

troubleshooting.title: High memory usage
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice high memory usage in the Cortex pods, check the resource limits and adjust them if necessary. You can view the resource usage with:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace cortex
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade command fails, check for any error messages and ensure that the chart version is compatible with your current installation. You can view the current release status with:
```bash
helm status cortex --namespace cortex
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
